flashcards.front: What is the Principle of Inclusion and Exclusion (PIE)?
flashcards.back: A counting technique that calculates the size of the union of multiple sets by adding individual set sizes and subtracting intersections to avoid double-counting.

flashcards.front: General PIE formula for n sets

flashcards.back: A permutation where no element appears in its original position. Denoted as Dₙ or !n.

flashcards.front: How many ways to select at least one object?
flashcards.back: Use PIE: Count total selections from all objects minus those selecting none. If each object can be chosen or not (2ⁿ total), then at least one = 2ⁿ - 1.

flashcards.front: What is the complement method in PIE?
flashcards.back: Instead of counting desired elements, count the complement (elements NOT satisfying conditions) using PIE, then subtract from total.

flashcards.front: Number of onto functions from m-element to n-element set
flashcards.back: n! × S(m,n) where S(m,n) is Stirling numbers of second kind, or using PIE: n^m - C(n,1)(n-1)^m + C(n,2)(n-2)^m - ... + (-1)ⁿ⁻¹C(n,n-1)(1)^m

flashcards.front: Euler's totient function φ(n) using PIE
flashcards.back: If n = p₁ᵅ¹ × p₂ᵅ² × ... × pkᵅᵏ, then φ(n) = n × Π(1 - 1/pᵢ) for i=1 to k. Derived using PIE to count numbers coprime to n.

flashcards.front: What is the Sieve of Eratosthenes related to PIE?
flashcards.back: An ancient algorithm using PIE principle to count primes up to n by sieving out multiples of each prime.

flashcards.front: PIE for counting integers divisible by at least one of given numbers
flashcards.back: If counting numbers ≤ N divisible by a or b: floor(N/a) + floor(N/b) - floor(N/lcm(a,b))

flashcards.front: What are binomial coefficients in PIE context?
flashcards.back: Used to select which intersections to consider. C(n,1) for single sets, C(n,2) for pairwise intersections, etc.
Because elements in intersections are counted multiple times (once in each set), so we subtract to count them exactly once.

flashcards.front: Why add back triple intersections after subtracting pairs?
flashcards.back: Elements in triple intersections were subtracted too many times (counted 3 times, subtracted 3 times = 0), so we add back once to count them correctly.
